metadata.title: Why Claude spends 5000 thinking tokens on "2+2" and 100 on a hard proof
metadata.purpose: An adaptive thinking layer decides dynamically whether and how much to think based on input complexity, then executes that decision before the final response.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro am_onyx) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.exclusions: internal attention mechanics, token economics, training details
metadata.visual_object: A horizontal timeline of thinking blocks of variable length (short for simple queries, long for complex ones) preceding each text answer.
metadata.hook: Why did Claude spend 5000 thinking tokens on one question but 100 on a harder one — and who decided?

beats.narration_text: A simple factual question ("What color is the sky?") vs. a complex derivation ("Show me the error in this proof").
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: How do you allocate compute to reasoning? A fixed budget wastes tokens on easy problems; an unlimited budget breaks the economics — so where does the amount come from?
Why did Claude spend 5000 thinking tokens on one question but 100 on a harder one — and who decided?
An adaptive thinking layer decides dynamically whether and how much to think based on input complexity, then executes that decision before the final response.
beats.visual: A horizontal timeline of thinking blocks of variable length (short for simple queries, long for complex ones) preceding each text answer.
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: "Fact: a square has 4 sides" (no thinking block) vs. "Sum of all primes below 100" (long thinking block, then answer).
An adaptive thinking layer decides dynamically whether and how much to think based on input complexity, then executes that decision before the final response.
